title: Lecture2 Part 2 Pricing 2024T1
body:
Lecture 2 – Part 2: Pricing of Forward and Futures

Hull Chapter 5  Recap from last week

• Arbitrage pricing strategy • We typically price derivatives by constructing replicating portfolios from assets with known prices.

• Forward/futures contracts • Contracts in which traders agree to trade an underlying asset, S, at some future time, T, at a price agreed today, 𝐹0

• 𝐹0 is chosen so that 𝑓0 = 0. • Later on, 𝑓𝑡 varies and can be either positive or negative.

Recap from last week

• Assumptions: • Complete markets • No transaction costs • Same constant interest rate for everybody • No taxes • Arbitrage-free prices

Free lunch

• An arbitrage exist when: • We can obtain a sure cash flow today and zero cash flows in the future. Alternatively, • We can have zero cash flows today and a certain positive cash flow in the future.

• When such trades are possible, prices are not in equilibrium, and we can trade on it.

Discrete compounding interest rates

• Annually compounded interest rates • Our interest payments arrived at the end of each year.

• We could have more frequent interest payments • Part of our annual interest payment would arrive before the end of the year, and we would start earning interest itself at that point.

• What is the relation between an annually compounded interest rates and a semi-annual one?

Continuous compounding interest rates

• What if we increase the annual frequency of payments to an arbitrary number n, then,

• Mathematically, most models in finance uses interest rates continuously compounded, i.e.. by setting n go to infinity.

• This is just a way to express interest rates • It does not change the level of interest rate or the amount of interests received. • We can always express any discrete compounded interest rates as a continuously compounded one • For instance, the relation between an annual compounded interest rate and continuously compounded one is:

• We will from now use continuously compounded interest rate, and simply denote it r

Clarifications on short selling

• Most replication strategies (arbitrage pricing) require short positions.

• Our complete markets assumption implies that one can short sell any asset without any frictions.

• Short selling involves selling an asset that is not owned (i.e., shorting a given asset), which is different from taking a short position in futures contracts.

Short selling assets

• To short sell an asset such as a stock, we need an “asset-bank” to lend us the asset • In practice, the role of “asset-bank” is fulfilled by brokers, banks, mutual funds or other large investors.

• Cash flows of shorting an asset at time 0 and closing our position at time T : • At time 0: Borrow the asset and sell it in the market. Cash flow: + 𝑆0 • At time T: Buy the asset back in the market to close the short position. Cash flow: −𝑆𝑇

The costs of shorting …

• Typically, short positions are charged a fee (the short-fee). This fee can be large particularly if short interest is high (number of short position in the asset).

• Opening short positions require collaterals to mitigate default risk.

• Brokers require to have a margin account (just like futures) which will be adjusted depending on daily gains and losses on the short position.

• Here, we will ignore the above frictions.

Dividends:

• If the stock shorted pays dividends during the life of the short position, we will need to pay the dividends to the broker to compensate the original owner of the stock for the dividend not received.  Arbitrage pricing of forwards

• To find the arbitrage-free price we want to replicate the cash flows of a long forward contract: • It costs nothing to enter the contract since 𝑓0 =0 • At time T, we pay 𝐹0 and receive the underlying worth 𝑆𝑇

• We can replicate 𝑆𝑇 by buying the underlying today, 𝑆0

• We can replicate– 𝐹0 at time T by borrowing the present value of 𝐹0 today, 𝐹0 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇

Arbitrage pricing of forwards

• We have constructed our replicating portfolio so that the cash flows at time T are equal

• In an arbitrage-free market, this implies that the cash flows at time 0 are also equal:

𝑟𝑇 What if: 𝐹0 (Quoted) > 𝑆0 𝑒 (Arbitrage-free)

• Suppose 𝐹0 (Quoted) > 𝑆0 𝑒 𝑟𝑇 (Arbitrage-free) • The delivery price of the quoted contract is “too high” • Let’s take a short position in the forward contract (sell the forward) • Take a long position in the replicating portfolio (buy the replicating portfolio)

• Cash flows:

• Time T cash flows are 0 𝑟𝑇 −𝑟𝑇 • 𝐹0 > 𝑆0 𝑒 𝐹0 𝑒 − 𝑆0 > 0: Positive cash flow at time 0 …. ☺

Cash-and-carry

• This trade is known as cash-and-carry arbitrage.

• Think of it as buying the underlying asset for cash in the spot market and carrying until the maturity.

• The reverse scheme, in which we short sell the asset in the spot market and buy it back at the delivery date is known as a reverse cash-andcarry arbitrage

Reverse cash-and-carry arbitrage

• Suppose 𝐹0 < 𝑆0 𝑒 𝑟𝑇 • The delivery price is “too low” • We take a long position in the forward contract • Take a short position in the replicating portfolio to cover it

• Cash flow consequences

• The cash flow consequences at T net out

• 𝐹0 < 𝑆0 𝑒 𝑟𝑇 𝑆0 − 𝐹0 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇 > 0: Positive cash flow at time 0  Forward contracts on dividend paying stocks

• Consider a forward contract written on a stock that pays a dividend I’ at time t between time 0 and T. • To replicate the long forward, we would borrow the present value 𝐼 = 𝑃𝑉 𝐼’ = 𝐼’𝑒 −𝑟𝑡 today and invest it until t.

• The arbitrage-free forward price is

Currency forwards

•An exchange rate is the price of one unit of foreign currency expressed in the local currency (i.e., AUD/Euro: number of AUD to get 1 Euro)

•Cash holdings in foreign currency will earn the foreign risk-free interest rate, 𝑟𝑓

•The other relevant variables are: • The underlying asset is one unit of the foreign currency per unit of local currency. • 𝑆0 is the current exchange rate, e.g. the price in AUD of 1 Euro • 𝐹0 is the forward exchange rate price in local currency 16  Replicating a long position in a currency forward

• If we long the forward contract, we pay 𝐹0 (in AUD) for one unit of the foreign currency (Euro) at time T (worth 𝑆𝑇 ). • We borrow the present value of 𝐹0 ,𝐹0 𝑒 −𝑟𝑇 , to replicate the negative cash flow at time T 𝑟𝑓 𝑇 • 1 Euro at time 0 grows to 𝑒 Euros at time T • To get exactly one unit of the foreign currency at time T we buy some fraction of it, 𝑀0 , today • 𝑀0 will earn interest at the foreign risk-free rate • At time T we will have 𝑀𝑇 = 𝑀0 𝑒 𝑟𝑓 𝑇 • We choose 𝑀0 so that we have exactly one unit of the foreign currency at time T

−𝑟𝑓 𝑇 −𝑟𝑓 𝑇 • Buying 𝑒 units of the foreign currency (Euro) at time 0 costs 𝑆0 𝑒  Replicating a long position in a currency forward

•Cash flows:

•Arbitrage-free forward price:

•This relationship is known as interest rate parity 18  What about a stock that pays a dividend yield rather than dollar dividends?

•Suppose the stock pays a continuous yield, q

•The trick is to continuously reinvest the dividend in the stock • If invest 𝑆0 in the stock at time 0, i.e. buy one stock 𝑞𝑇 • It becomes 𝑆𝑇 𝑒 at time T because of reinvested dividends. • Since the cash flow we want to replicate is 𝑆𝑇 , we buy a bit less than −𝑞𝑇 1 share. We need to buy 𝑒 unit of the stock. • If we buy 𝑒 −𝑞𝑇 units of 𝑆0 it grows to 𝑆𝑇 𝑒 −𝑞𝑇 𝑒 𝑞𝑇 = 𝑆𝑇 at time T • This is very similar to what we did for the currency forward

What about a stock that pays a certain dividend yield rather than a certain dollar amount?

•Cash flow consequences:

•Arbitrage-free forward price:

•This strategy means continuously buying additional fractions of the stock as dividends are paid out.  Commodity forwards

• The storage costs incurred when holding an asset, e.g. storage costs, will be reflected in the price of commodity forwards (otherwise there is an arbitrage). • Storage costs is as a dividend (except that U = - I). • If the present value of the costs are U, we have (from slide 17):

• If the costs are expressed as a proportion, u, of the spot price, we have (from slide 22):

• Sometimes there is value associated with holding physically the asset. This is known as a convenience yield, y. • For instance, if the physical good is an input of some production process. • We can interpret such values as a known yield, the convenience yield.

(𝑟−𝑦)𝑇 𝐹0 = 𝑆0 𝑒  What if the asset is a commodity with storage cost (U)?

• Shorting in such a case would be equivalent to a storage facility for the lender. • The “asset-bank” (the lender) may “store” the asset with us (the shorter) by lending us the commodity. • The lender must pay us U (i.e., the PV of the cost he would incur to keep the commodity)

• If we charged nothing there would be an arbitrage profit for lenders of the commodity: • At time 0: • Borrow: +𝑆0 • Buy the asset: - 𝑆0 • Lend it to a short seller (us) that returns it at T • Take a short forward position maturing at T, with 𝐾 = 𝐹0 = (𝑆0 + 𝑈)𝑒 𝑟𝑇 • Time 0 cash flows: +𝑆0 – 𝑆0 = 0 • At time T: • Get the asset back from the short seller and use it to close the forward contract, receive 𝐹0 = (𝑆0 + 𝑈)𝑒 𝑟𝑇 • Pay bank loan (with interest) of 𝑆0 𝑒 𝑟𝑇 • Time T cash flows: 𝑆0 + 𝑈 𝑒 𝑟𝑇 − 𝑆0 𝑒 𝑟𝑇 = 𝑈𝑒 𝑟𝑇 > 0 an (an ar

Generalizing forward price formula for any asset

• We are typically interested in the net cost of carrying the replicating portfolio, the cost-of-carry

• We need to take into account: • The interest paid to finance the purchase of the asset, r • Any foreign interest we might earn, 𝑟𝑓 • Any storage costs we may incur, u • Any dividend the asset may yield, q

• The cost of carry, c, is calculated as 𝑐 = 𝑟 – 𝑟𝑓 + 𝑢 – 𝑞 • Typically, some of these variables equal zero • Stock in local currency that does not pay dividend: c = r

• The convenience yield, which is not real cash flow, is not included in the cost-of-carry.

• We end up with the general formula for the forward price:

Delivery

• The holder of a short position in a forward contract can choose when to make delivery (within some limits) • In these cases, we assume that the delivery date is chosen optimally when calculating the futures price. • We can get a sense for the optimal time of delivery by looking at how the futures price reacts to a change in the delivery date

• The forward price increases with T if 𝑐 > 𝑦 (as 𝐹0 > 0) • This is because it is expensive to hold the replicating portfolio when 𝑐 > 𝑦, i.e. when the costof-carry is higher than the convenience yield.

• The holder of a short position will optimally choose to close such an expensive position by making delivery as early as possible.

• The reverse is true when 𝑐 < 𝑦.  Pricing of futures v.s. forwards

• If the interest rate is deterministic, the forward price and futures price are equal

• What if interest rate change over time?

• Assume that interest rate is positively correlated to underlying asset price 𝐶𝑜𝑣 𝑟, 𝑆𝑡 > 0

𝑟 v 𝑆𝑡 v 𝐹𝑡 = 𝑆𝑡 𝑒 𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 • Gain from daily settlement grows the margin account which pays higher interest 𝑟 𝑇−𝑡 𝑟 v 𝑆𝑡 v 𝐹𝑡 = 𝑆𝑡 𝑒 • Loss from daily settlement decreases margin balance but interest is low. • Overall, you prefer having more money in margin account when interest is high. • Thus, future price will be higher than forward price.
